sections.heading: BARMM trails on every antenatal care measure
sections.body: In 2025, BARMM stood out because it was low across the full antenatal care process, not just one measure. Skilled antenatal care was 57.2 percent, 4+ visits were 43.3 percent, 8+ visits were 14.0 percent, iron supplementation was 62.6 percent, and neonatal tetanus protection was 54.8 percent. Some regions may lag on one part of care, but BARMM was lowest on all five measures. This points to a broad problem with getting care and staying in care, rather than weakness in only one indicator.

sections.heading: Seeing a skilled provider does not always lead to 8+ visits
sections.body: Several regions had high levels of skilled antenatal care, but far fewer pregnancies reached 8+ visits. Caraga had 87.9 percent skilled antenatal care and 94.3 percent with 4+ visits, yet only 29.3 percent reached 8+ visits. Davao Region had 90.9 percent skilled antenatal care and 91.1 percent with 4+ visits, but 8+ visits were 27.3 percent. This pattern suggests that early or basic care is much more common than completing the fuller visit schedule.

sections.heading: The biggest gains happened in different parts of care
sections.body: From 2022 to 2025, the largest regional gains showed up in different parts of antenatal care. Zamboanga Peninsula¹ had the biggest increase in skilled antenatal care, rising 16.4 points from 70.7 to 87.1 percent. BARMM had the biggest gain in 4+ visits, up 15.4 points from 27.9 to 43.3 percent, while Central Visayas had the biggest gain in 8+ visits, up 29.2 points from 35.1 to 64.3 percent. Progress was spread across different regions, depending on which part of care is measured.

sections.heading: Tetanus protection fell in some regions with high care contact
sections.body: Some regions had strong antenatal care contact, but neonatal tetanus protection did not keep pace. Eastern Visayas still had 93.0 percent skilled antenatal care in 2025, but neonatal tetanus protection fell from 94.9 percent in 2022 to 77.2 percent in 2025. Cagayan Valley also had 88.4 percent skilled antenatal care, while neonatal tetanus protection declined from 94.4 to 81.2 percent. This gap matters because tetanus protection does not simply rise and fall with overall antenatal contact.

sections.heading: National improvement was clearest for 8+ visits
sections.body: Nationally, antenatal care changed most clearly in how many visits pregnancies received. For live births and stillbirths, skilled antenatal care was 87.3 percent in 2025, 4+ visits were 84.8 percent, and iron supplementation was 87.7 percent. These were all only modestly higher than in 2022. The 8+ visit measure rose much more, from 29.1 to 39.9 percent. The main national change was therefore not whether women had any skilled contact, but whether more pregnancies reached the higher visit threshold.

sections.heading: Mothers under 20 are lower on later care measures
sections.body: In 2025, mothers younger than 20 had lower antenatal care results than older mothers. Skilled antenatal care was 84.6 percent for mothers under 20, compared with 87.8 percent for ages 20 to 34 and 86.8 percent for ages 35 to 49. The gap was sharper for 8+ visits: mothers under 20 were at 25.8 percent, compared with 42.8 percent among ages 20 to 34. Neonatal tetanus protection was also lower for mothers under 20 at 69.4 percent.

sections.heading: The urban advantage is mainly in reaching 8+ visits
sections.body: Urban-rural differences were visible in 2025, but they were not equally large for every antenatal care measure. Skilled antenatal care differed by 2.6 points, with urban areas at 88.6 percent and rural areas at 86.0 percent. The gap was much larger for 8+ visits, where urban areas reached 47.3 percent compared with 32.6 percent in rural areas. Where mothers live therefore matters most for reaching the higher visit threshold, not simply for seeing a skilled provider.

sections.heading: Education gaps are wide, especially for continued care
sections.body: In 2025, antenatal care differed widely by education, both in first contact and in continued care. Skilled antenatal care was 95.0 percent among mothers with college education, compared with 49.8 percent among mothers with no education and 76.1 percent among those with Grades 1-6. For 8+ visits, the contrast was also large: 53.2 percent for college and 54.2 percent for post-secondary, compared with 24.1 percent for no education and 14.7 percent for Grades 1-6. This pattern shows that education gaps affect both getting care and continuing through more visits.

sections.heading: Wealth gaps are much larger for 8+ visits
sections.body: Wealth differences show up across antenatal care, but they are much larger for 8+ visits. Skilled antenatal care was 95.6 percent in the highest wealth quintile and 80.0 percent in the lowest. For 8+ visits, the highest quintile reached 67.5 percent, while the lowest was only 20.7 percent. The gap between the highest and lowest quintiles was therefore 15.6 points for skilled care, but 46.8 points for 8+ visits. This shows that sustained care is where wealth differences become most pronounced.

takeaways: Nationally, antenatal care is high for skilled contact and 4+ visits, but 8+ visits are still much lower.
Regional differences are sharpest when care needs to continue, and BARMM is lowest on all five antenatal care measures.
The largest gains from 2022 to 2025 are spread across different regions and indicators, rather than centered on one region.

Gaps by wealth and residence are largest for 8+ visits, showing that continued care is less evenly shared than basic contact in those dimensions. Education gaps are also large for 8+ visits, though the no-education to college gap is larger for skilled care than for 8+ visits.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
